title: Spartacus w/ The Serpents and the Wolves Expansion

walkthrough.title: Choose Your House
walkthrough.text: Alright, let's get Spartacus set up. Each player rolls a die. The player with the highest roll picks their House Card first and chooses a Gladiator figure. That player also receives the Host token. Going clockwise, each remaining player picks a House Card and figure. Each House has different strengths, starting assets, and special rules printed right on the card, so take a moment to read yours. You also get your matching House tokens in your House's color.

walkthrough.title: Choose a Game Type
walkthrough.text: Before dealing anything out, your group needs to pick a game length. There are three options. Quick mode starts everyone at 7 Influence and usually takes under two hours. This is great for your first game. Standard mode starts at 4 Influence and takes two to three hours. Advanced mode starts at just 1 Influence and can run over three hours as each House claws its way up from obscurity. Set your Influence tracker on your House Card to the chosen starting number.

walkthrough.title: Separate Starting Assets
walkthrough.text: Find the white-bordered Starting Gladiators and Starting Slaves in the Market Deck. There are 8 of each. Pull them all out. Shuffle the Starting Gladiators into their own pile and shuffle the Starting Slaves into their own pile. These are dealt separately from the main decks.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Gladiators and Slaves
walkthrough.text: Each House Card lists how many starting Gladiators and Slaves you receive. Deal each player their starting number from the shuffled Starting Gladiator and Starting Slave piles. Place your Gladiators to the right of your House Card and your Slaves to the left. Any leftover Starting Gladiators and Slaves get shuffled into the main Market Deck.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Starting Guards
walkthrough.text: Each House Card also lists a starting number of Guards. Find Guard cards from the Intrigue Deck and deal the correct number to each player. Starting Guards must be placed face up on the table as Assets, not held in your hand. Guards protect your House from Schemes played by rivals.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Starting Coins
walkthrough.text: Give each player the number of starting Coins listed on their House Card. Place them on the Treasury section of your House Card. The remaining Coins form the Bank. You can designate one player as banker or everyone can share the responsibility. Important: Coins can change hands between players at any time during the game with no restrictions.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Decks
walkthrough.text: Now shuffle the Market Deck thoroughly, including any leftover Starting Gladiators and Slaves you mixed in. Shuffle the Intrigue Deck thoroughly as well. Place both decks face down on the table. Leave space next to each deck for a face-up discard pile.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Arena and Betting Board
walkthrough.text: Place the Arena board in the center of the table where everyone can reach it. The Arena is a hex grid where gladiatorial combat takes place. Next to it, place the Betting Board, which has spaces for Victory, Injury, and Decapitation wagers. Make sure the dice are nearby too. There are three colors: red for Attack, black for Defence, and blue for Speed.

walkthrough.title: Organize the Tokens
walkthrough.text: Sort the tokens into piles near the center of the table. You need Favour tokens, Champion tokens, Injury tokens, and Coin tokens easily accessible. The Favour and Champion tokens are earned by Gladiators who win Arena matches. Injury tokens mark wounded Gladiators or Slaves.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Play
walkthrough.text: Take one last look at your House Card. Note your special rules, your Hand Size for Intrigue cards based on your current Influence, and your starting assets laid out around the card. The player with the Host token will go first during the Intrigue Phase. The goal is to be the first and only House to reach 12 Influence. Let the scheming begin!

Each player takes their matching House tokens

Set Influence tracker on House Card

summary.title: Ready to Play

Host token holder goes first in Intrigue Phase
Goal: be the first and only House to reach 12 Influence

walkthrough.title: Turn Structure Overview
walkthrough.text: Each game turn in Spartacus has four parts, and they always happen in this order. First is Upkeep, where you refresh your cards, heal injuries, and balance your books. Second is the Intrigue Phase, where you draw cards, play Schemes, and use special abilities. Third is the Market Phase, where you buy, sell, and trade assets and bid at auction. Fourth is the Arena Phase, where gladiators fight for glory and Influence. After the Arena Phase, a new turn begins with Upkeep again. The game continues until one player ends a phase as the only Dominus with 12 or more Influence.

walkthrough.title: Upkeep: Refresh, Heal, and Pay
walkthrough.text: At the start of every turn, all players handle Upkeep simultaneously. First, Refresh Cards: flip any uninjured Exhausted cards face up. They are now Ready and can be used again. Second, Heal Injuries: for each card with an Injury token, roll a die. On a 4, 5, or 6, remove the Injury token and Refresh the card. On a 2 or 3, the card stays Injured this turn. On a 1, that Gladiator or Slave has died from their injury and goes to the Market discard pile. Third, Balance the Ledgers: you earn 1 Coin from the Bank for each Ready Slave you own, and you must pay 1 Coin to the Bank for each Ready Gladiator. If you cannot pay for all your Gladiators, you must discard one Gladiator for each unpaid Coin, setting them free.

walkthrough.title: Intrigue Phase: Draw Cards
walkthrough.text: At the start of the Intrigue Phase, all players simultaneously draw 3 Intrigue Cards. This may push your hand over your Hand Size limit, and that is fine for now. Your Hand Size is printed on your House Card under your current Influence level. You can hold more cards during your turn, but you must be at or under your Hand Size when you end your Intrigue turn. If the Intrigue Deck runs out, shuffle the discard pile and reuse it.

walkthrough.title: Intrigue Phase: Taking Your Turn
walkthrough.text: Starting with the player who holds the Host token and going clockwise, each player takes an Intrigue turn. On your turn, you can do three things in any order: Cash In cards for Coins, use card or House special abilities, and play Schemes. You can do as many of these as you want. Cashing In means discarding an Intrigue Card face up and taking its listed Coin Value from the Bank. Special abilities on your Slaves, Gladiators, or House Card that say they work during the Intrigue Phase can be activated by Exhausting the card. When you are done, make sure your hand is at or under your Hand Size, then play passes left.

walkthrough.title: Playing Schemes
walkthrough.text: Schemes are the heart of the Intrigue Phase. Each Scheme card has an Influence requirement. If your current Influence equals or exceeds that number, you can play it. Declare a Target Dominus, which can be yourself or another player depending on the card. The card's effects happen immediately if nobody Foils it. If you do not have enough Influence to play a Scheme on your own, you can ask other players for Support. Their Influence is temporarily added to yours for the purpose of playing that one Scheme. You do not have to show them the Scheme, and you do not have to tell the truth about what it does. The granting of support is final once given. Support does not change anyone's actual Influence.

walkthrough.title: Foiling Schemes and Using Guards
walkthrough.text: When someone plays a Scheme, other players can try to stop it. Reaction cards that say Foil a Scheme can be played immediately. Reactions have their own Influence requirement but cannot receive Support from other players. Guard cards are a special type of Reaction that can only Foil Schemes targeting your own House. To use a Guard, discard it and roll a die: on 4, 5, or 6, the Scheme is Foiled. On 1, 2, or 3, the Guard fails but is still discarded. You can try additional Guards if you have them. If a Scheme is Foiled, none of its effects happen, including any costs. Guards can be held in your hand or deployed face up on the table as Assets.

walkthrough.title: Market Phase: Open Market
walkthrough.text: During the Open Market, there is no turn order. Players can freely buy, sell, and trade Asset cards with each other. Gladiators, Slaves, Equipment, and Guards can all change hands. You can also sell Assets to the Bank for their printed Coin Value. All deals are permitted, and players can negotiate any terms involving Assets and Coins. However, Intrigue Cards can never be traded or sold. This is the only time Assets can change hands between players. When everyone is done dealing, all players pick up their Coins and conceal them. This signals Open Market is over.

walkthrough.title: Market Phase: Auction
walkthrough.text: After the Open Market, the Auction begins. Deal a number of Market Deck cards face down equal to the number of players. Reveal the first card and all players bid simultaneously using concealed bidding. Place your bid in your closed fist and extend it over the table. Everyone reveals at once. The highest bidder pays the Bank and claims the Asset. If players tie for highest, only the tied players bid again in a new round, adding to their previous bid. If all remaining bidders bid zero in any round, it is a Failed Auction and the card is discarded. Continue revealing and bidding on each card in order.

walkthrough.title: Market Phase: Bid for Hosting
walkthrough.text: After all Auction cards are resolved, players bid for the right to Host the next Arena Phase using the same concealed bidding method. The winner pays the Bank and takes the Host marker. This is critical because the Host gains 1 Influence and controls who fights in the Arena. If the bid results in a Failed Auction, all remaining tied bidders roll a die and the highest roller becomes Host. Someone always has to Host. After this bid, all players return remaining Coins to their Treasury and the Arena Phase begins.

walkthrough.title: Arena Phase: Host Gains Influence and Issues Invitations
walkthrough.text: The Host immediately gains 1 Influence for presiding over the Games. Then the Host must fill two spots in the Arena by issuing Invitations one at a time. The Host can invite any player, including themselves. When invited, a player selects a Gladiator or Slave, places their figure on the Arena hex marked I or II, and commits their Combatant's card along with up to one Weapon, one Armour, and one Special Equipment card. These choices are final once committed. If a player declines an Invitation, they lose 1 Influence, and the Host invites someone else. The Host can solicit bribes and deals from players wanting or avoiding Invitations.

walkthrough.title: Arena Phase: Tribute and Wagers
walkthrough.text: After both Combatants are committed, Tribute is paid. Each Favour token on a Combatant earns its owner 2 Coins from the Bank. A Champion token earns 6 Coins. Then all players place Wagers simultaneously. There are three types of wagers. Victory pays 1 to 1: bet that a specific Combatant wins. Injury pays 2 to 1: bet that the match ends in an Injury. Decapitation pays 2 to 1: bet that someone gets Decapitated. Each player can bet 1 to 3 Coins on any wager, placing Coins on the Betting Board capped with their House token. Players whose Gladiators are fighting may not bet against their own Combatant.

walkthrough.title: Combat: Attributes and Dice Pools
walkthrough.text: Every Combatant has three attributes, each represented by colored dice. Attack is red dice, used when attacking. Defence is black dice, used when defending. Speed is blue dice, used for initiative and movement. The total number of dice across all three pools represents your Combatant's health. As your Combatant takes Wounds, you surrender dice from any pool you choose. When any single pool reaches zero, your Combatant is defeated. This is the core tension of combat: do you sacrifice Attack to stay alive, give up Defence to keep hitting hard, or lose Speed and risk being outmaneuvered?

walkthrough.title: Combat: Initiative, Movement, and Attacking
walkthrough.text: Each Combat Round starts with an Initiative Roll. Both players roll their Speed dice and total the results. The higher total wins Initiative and chooses to go first or second. Each player then Moves and Attacks in the order they choose: you can Move then Attack, or Attack then Move. Movement lets you move a number of hexes up to your current Speed dice count. Attacks are resolved through Opposed Dice Rolling. The attacker rolls their current Attack dice, the defender rolls their current Defence dice. Both line up their results highest to lowest into Dice Strings and compare position by position. If the Attack die beats the Defence die at that position, it causes a Wound. Ties go to the Defender. Any unopposed Attack dice on 3 or higher also cause Wounds.

walkthrough.title: Combat: Wounds, the Rule of One, and Defeat
walkthrough.text: When a Combatant takes Wounds, they must surrender that many dice from any of their pools. Here is a crucial rule: No single pool can be reduced below 1 die until all pools have only 1 die each. This is the Rule of One. It prevents a player from dumping all Wounds into one stat. Once all pools are at 1, any further Wound causes defeat. There are three levels of defeat. Yield happens when one pool hits zero while others remain. The Combatant is at the mercy of the Host but suffers no lasting harm beyond the loss. Injury happens when two pools hit zero. The card is Exhausted and marked with an Injury token. Decapitation happens when all three pools hit zero. The Combatant is dead and goes to the discard pile.

walkthrough.title: Victory, Favour, Champions, and Life or Death
walkthrough.text: After combat, the winning Dominus gains 1 Influence, and the victorious Combatant receives a Favour token. When a Combatant earns its third Favour token, it becomes a Champion. Return the Favour tokens and place a Champion token instead. Owning a Champion grants +1 Influence; losing one costs -1 Influence. The Host then decides the defeated Combatant's fate with Thumbs Up or Thumbs Down. Thumbs Up returns the Combatant and Equipment to their owner. Thumbs Down executes the Combatant and discards it. Executing a Combatant with Favour tokens costs the Host 1 Influence per token. You may never execute a Champion. After settling wagers with the Bank, the turn ends and a new one begins with Upkeep.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: The goal is to reach 12 Influence. But timing matters. If, at the end of any Phase (Intrigue, Market, or Arena), exactly one player has 12 or more Influence, that player wins. If two or more players end a phase at 12 Influence simultaneously, a Tournament decides the winner. In a two-player tie, both select and equip a Combatant and fight a single Arena match. In a three-player tie, all three total their Gladiator and Equipment Coin Values. The two lowest fight first, then the winner fights the remaining player. In a four-player tie, the two lowest totals fight and the two highest fight, then the winners face off. Reaching 12 during a phase does not guarantee victory because opponents may knock you back down before the phase ends.

summary.title: Turn Structure Overview
bullets: Each turn: Upkeep, Intrigue Phase, Market Phase, Arena Phase
All four phases happen every turn in order
Game ends when exactly one player has 12+ Influence at end of any phase

summary.title: Upkeep: Refresh, Heal, and Pay
bullets: Refresh: flip uninjured Exhausted cards face up (Ready)
Heal: roll die per Injured card (4-6 heals, 2-3 stays Injured, 1 = death)
Ledgers: +1 Coin per Ready Slave, -1 Coin per Ready Gladiator
Cannot pay for Gladiator = must discard one per unpaid Coin

summary.title: Intrigue Phase: Draw Cards
bullets: All players draw 3 Intrigue Cards simultaneously
May temporarily exceed Hand Size during your turn
Must be at or under Hand Size when ending your Intrigue turn
Hand Size is listed on House Card under current Influence

summary.title: Intrigue Phase: Taking Your Turn
bullets: Turn order: Host first, then clockwise
Cash In cards for their listed Coin Value
Use card or House special abilities (Exhaust to activate)
Play Schemes against rivals or yourself

summary.title: Playing Schemes
bullets: Each Scheme has an Influence requirement to play
Declare a Target Dominus; effects happen immediately if not Foiled
Can ask other players for Support to meet Influence requirement
Support does not change actual Influence; you need not reveal the Scheme

summary.title: Foiling Schemes and Using Guards
bullets: Reaction cards can Foil Schemes; Reactions cannot receive Support
Guards Foil Schemes targeting you: discard and roll (4-6 success, 1-3 fail)
Failed Guard is still discarded; can attempt multiple Guards
Foiled Scheme = none of its effects or costs happen

summary.title: Market Phase: Open Market
bullets: No turn order; free trading of Assets and Coins
Can sell Assets to Bank for printed Coin Value
Only time Assets can change hands between players
Intrigue Cards may never be traded or sold

summary.title: Market Phase: Auction
bullets: Deal Market cards face down equal to player count, reveal one at a time
Concealed simultaneous bidding; highest bidder pays Bank and claims Asset
Tied bids: only tied players bid again, adding to previous bids
All bid zero in any round = Failed Auction, card discarded

summary.title: Market Phase: Bid for Hosting
bullets: Concealed bid for Host marker; winner pays Bank
Host gains 1 Influence and controls Arena Invitations
Failed Auction: tied bidders roll off; someone must Host
After bidding, all players return Coins to Treasury

summary.title: Arena Phase: Host Gains Influence and Issues Invitations
bullets: Host gains +1 Influence immediately
Host invites two players (one at a time) to fight in the Arena
Host may invite themselves; can solicit bribes for Invitations
Declining an Invitation costs 1 Influence

summary.title: Arena Phase: Tribute and Wagers
bullets: Favour tokens = 2 Coins tribute; Champion token = 6 Coins
Victory wager: 1-to-1 payout on a specific Combatant winning
Injury wager: 2-to-1 payout if match ends in Injury
Decapitation wager: 2-to-1 payout if Combatant is Decapitated
Max 3 Coins per wager; fighters cannot bet against their own Combatant

summary.title: Combat: Attributes and Dice Pools
bullets: Attack (red dice), Defence (black dice), Speed (blue dice)
Total dice across all pools = Combatant health
Wounds = surrender dice from any pool of your choice
Any pool reaching zero = defeat

summary.title: Combat: Initiative, Movement, and Attacking
bullets: Initiative: both roll Speed dice, higher total chooses first or second
Move up to current Speed dice count in hexes
Opposed Dice Rolling: ATK vs DEF, line up highest to lowest
ATK > DEF at same position = Wound; ties go to Defender
Unopposed ATK dice on 3+ = automatic Wound

summary.title: Combat: Wounds, the Rule of One, and Defeat
bullets: Surrender dice equal to Wounds taken from any pool
Rule of One: no pool below 1 die until all pools are at 1
Yield: 1 pool at zero (mercy of Host, no lasting effect)
Injury: 2 pools at zero (Exhausted + Injury token)
Decapitation: all 3 pools at zero (Combatant dies, discarded)

summary.title: Victory, Favour, Champions, and Life or Death
bullets: Winner gains +1 Influence; Combatant gets a Favour token
3rd Favour = Champion token (+1 Influence to owner)
Losing a Champion = -1 Influence
Host decides Thumbs Up (return) or Thumbs Down (execute defeated)
Executing Favour-holder costs Host 1 Influence per Favour; Champions cannot be executed

summary.title: Winning the Game
bullets: Win: be the only player at 12+ Influence at end of any phase
Multiple players at 12: Tournament (Arena fights) decides winner
Reaching 12 mid-phase does not guarantee victory; rivals can knock you down
Tournament tiebreaker: total Gladiator + Equipment Coin Values

walkthrough.title: Welcome & Meet the Players
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first turn with three players. Pete is playing House Batiatus, Sally is playing House Solonius, and John is playing House Glaber. We are using the Quick game type, so everyone starts at 7 Influence. Pete won the initial roll and holds the Host token. Let's see how a full turn plays out from Upkeep through the Arena.

walkthrough.title: Upkeep: First Turn
walkthrough.text: Since this is the very first turn, all cards are already face up and Ready. Nobody has any Injured Gladiators or Slaves yet. For Balance the Ledgers, each player counts their Ready Gladiators and Ready Slaves. Pete has 2 Gladiators and 2 Slaves, so he pays 2 Coins for Gladiators and earns 2 Coins from Slaves. Net zero. Sally has 1 Gladiator and 3 Slaves, so she pays 1 and earns 3, netting 2 Coins from the Bank. John has 2 Gladiators and 1 Slave, paying 2 and earning 1, so he pays 1 Coin to the Bank. Already you can see how Slave-heavy Houses generate income while Gladiator-heavy Houses burn through it.

walkthrough.title: Intrigue Phase: Drawing Cards
walkthrough.text: All three players draw 3 Intrigue Cards from the deck. Pete looks at his hand and sees a Guard, a Scheme called Black Market Profits that requires 7 Influence, and a Slave with a useful special ability. Sally has two Schemes and a Reaction card. John has a Guard, a Scheme, and a card he plans to Cash In for Coins. Remember, you can hold more cards than your Hand Size during your turn, but you must trim down before ending it.

walkthrough.title: Pete's Intrigue Turn
walkthrough.text: Pete goes first because he holds the Host token. He looks at Black Market Profits, which requires 7 Influence and says Gain 5 Coins. Pete has exactly 7 Influence, so he can play it. He declares himself as the Target Dominus and places the card on the table. Nobody plays a Reaction to Foil it, so Pete gains 5 Coins from the Bank. Nice haul. Pete then deploys his Guard card face up as an Asset on the table for future protection. He is at his Hand Size, so his turn is done.

walkthrough.title: Sally's Intrigue Turn
walkthrough.text: Sally has a Scheme that would give her +1 Influence, but it requires 9 Influence to play and she only has 7. She asks John for Support. John says sure, for 2 Coins. Sally agrees and pays John 2 Coins. With John's 7 Influence added to her 7, Sally now has 14 combined Influence for this Scheme only. She plays it, targeting herself, and gains +1 Influence, moving to 8. John's Influence does not change. Sally then Cashes In her other Scheme for 2 Coins since she needs to get to Hand Size anyway.

walkthrough.title: John's Mistake: Wasting a Scheme
walkthrough.text: John has a Scheme that costs the Target Dominus 1 Influence. He wants to knock Sally back down, so he plays it targeting Sally. But wait, Sally has a Reaction card in her hand that says Foil a Scheme. She plays it immediately, and John's Scheme is completely negated. The Scheme goes to the discard pile with no effect. John wasted his card and his turn playing it. He should have Cashed it In for Coins instead. Always consider whether your target might have a Foil in hand before launching a Scheme, especially when you could just take the guaranteed Coins instead.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Check Defenses Before Scheming
walkthrough.text: The takeaway here is that Schemes are powerful but risky. Before playing a Scheme, look at your target's table. Do they have Guards deployed? Do they have cards in hand that could be Reactions? If your target has strong defenses, sometimes it is better to Cash In the Scheme for guaranteed Coins rather than gamble on it going through. The Coins are always useful, but a Foiled Scheme gives you nothing.

walkthrough.title: Open Market
walkthrough.text: Open Market begins. John is low on Coins after Upkeep and the failed Scheme. He asks if anyone wants to buy one of his Guards for 3 Coins. Pete could use another Guard and agrees. John hands over the Guard card and Pete pays 3 Coins. Sally offers to sell a Slave to Pete for 4 Coins, but Pete declines, saying the Bank would only give 2 for it. They haggle briefly but no deal is made. Everyone picks up their Coins and conceals them, ending the Open Market.

walkthrough.title: John's Mistake: Revealing Wealth at Auction
walkthrough.text: The Auction begins. Three Market Deck cards are dealt face down. The first card is revealed: a strong Gladiator with 4 ATK, 3 DEF, and 3 SPD. Everyone bids. John gets excited and bids 6 Coins, his entire treasury. He wins the Gladiator, but now everyone at the table knows he is completely broke. When the second card is revealed, a useful piece of Equipment, John cannot bid at all. And worse, when it comes time to bid for Hosting, he has nothing left. Remember the rulebook says to keep your Coins concealed until after Hosting bids. John tipped his hand by spending everything.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Budget for Hosting
walkthrough.text: Never spend all your Coins in the Auction. Always reserve some for the Hosting bid. The Host gains 1 Influence, controls who fights in the Arena, and can collect bribes from other players. Hosting is one of the most powerful positions in the game. If you blow your entire budget on an Asset, you hand that power to someone else for free. A good rule of thumb: set aside at least 3 to 5 Coins for the Hosting bid before spending anything at Auction.

walkthrough.title: Bid for Hosting
walkthrough.text: Pete bids 4 Coins, Sally bids 3, and John bids 0 because he is broke. Pete wins the Host marker and pays 4 Coins to the Bank. Pete will host the Arena this turn and gain 1 Influence. All players return remaining Coins to their Treasury.

walkthrough.title: Arena Phase: Pete Hosts
walkthrough.text: Pete gains 1 Influence as Host, moving from 7 to 8. Now he needs to invite two players to fight. Pete decides to invite John first because John has a weaker Gladiator and Pete wants to see John's new purchase tested. John accepts and places his new Gladiator on hex I with a Weapon card. Pete then invites Sally. Sally considers declining because her Gladiator is average, but declining costs 1 Influence and she does not want to drop from 8 to 7. She accepts and places her Gladiator on hex II.

walkthrough.title: Tribute and Wagers
walkthrough.text: Neither Combatant has Favour or Champion tokens yet, so no Tribute is paid. All three players place wagers. Pete bets 2 Coins on John's Gladiator to win, since John's Gladiator has better stats. Sally bets 2 Coins on Victory for her own Gladiator because she believes in her fighter and cannot bet against herself. John bets 3 Coins on Injury, hoping the fight will be long and brutal.

walkthrough.title: Combat Plays Out
walkthrough.text: Initiative is rolled. John's Gladiator has 3 Speed dice and rolls a total of 10. Sally's has 2 Speed dice and rolls 7. John wins Initiative and chooses to go first. He moves into an adjacent hex and attacks. John rolls 4 red Attack dice: 6, 4, 3, 1. Sally rolls 3 black Defence dice: 5, 3, 2. They line up Dice Strings. Position 1: John's 6 vs Sally's 5, Wound. Position 2: John's 4 vs Sally's 3, Wound. Position 3: John's 3 vs Sally's 2, Wound. John's leftover 1 is unopposed but below 3, so no extra Wound. Sally takes 3 Wounds and must surrender 3 dice. She drops 1 ATK, 1 DEF, and 1 SPD. The fight continues for several more rounds until Sally's Gladiator is reduced to Yield.

walkthrough.title: John's Mistake: Choosing Wrong Dice to Surrender
walkthrough.text: In an earlier round, John took 2 Wounds and decided to surrender both from his Defence pool to keep his Attack high. While this seems aggressive, it left his Gladiator with only 1 DEF die for the rest of the fight. Every attack from Sally started causing Wounds because John could barely block anything. A smarter approach would have been to spread the losses, maybe dropping 1 ATK and 1 SPD, keeping a more balanced fighter. When surrendering dice, think about what you need most for the rest of the fight, not just the next round.

walkthrough.title: Victory and the Host's Power
walkthrough.text: John's Gladiator wins. John gains 1 Influence (moving to 8) and his Gladiator receives a Favour token. Wagers are settled. Pete bet on John to win and collects his 2 Coins back plus 2 from the Bank. Sally loses her 2-Coin bet on herself. John bet on Injury, but Sally's Gladiator only Yielded, not Injured, so John loses his 3-Coin bet. Pete, as Host, must now decide Sally's Gladiator's fate. Thumbs Up returns the Gladiator to Sally. Thumbs Down executes it. Pete gives Thumbs Up, keeping the peace. The turn is over.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have now seen a complete turn of Spartacus: Upkeep, Intrigue, Market, and Arena. You watched Pete use Schemes effectively, saw John waste a Scheme that got Foiled, learned why you should budget for Hosting, and observed how combat dice work with Wounds and Dice Strings. The three most important lessons: always consider if your Scheme will get Foiled before playing it, never blow your entire budget at Auction, and spread your Wound losses across dice pools to keep your Combatant balanced.

walkthrough.title: Before You Enter the Arena
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics on when to scheme, when to save, and how to manage your Gladiator stable.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to card types, special abilities, and game terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Gloria Victis!

summary.title: Welcome & Meet the Players
bullets: Scripted tutorial with 3 players: Pete (Batiatus), Sally (Solonius), John (Glaber)
Quick game type, all starting at 7 Influence
Covers a complete turn: Upkeep through Arena

summary.title: Upkeep: First Turn
bullets: First turn: all cards already Ready, no Injuries to heal
Balance Ledgers: +1 Coin per Slave, -1 Coin per Gladiator
Slave-heavy Houses earn income; Gladiator-heavy Houses pay upkeep

summary.title: Intrigue Phase: Drawing Cards
bullets: All players draw 3 Intrigue Cards simultaneously
May temporarily exceed Hand Size during your turn
Plan which cards to play, Cash In, or keep

summary.title: Pete's Intrigue Turn
bullets: Pete plays Black Market Profits (7 Influence, gain 5 Coins)
Scheme succeeds because nobody Foils it
Pete deploys a Guard as an Asset for future protection

summary.title: Sally's Intrigue Turn
bullets: Sally's Scheme requires 9 Influence but she only has 7
John supports her for 2 Coins, temporarily adding his 7 Influence
Scheme succeeds; Sally gains +1 Influence to 8; John's Influence unchanged

summary.title: John's Mistake: Wasting a Scheme
bullets: John plays a Scheme targeting Sally; Sally Foils it with a Reaction
John's Scheme is discarded with no effect
Should have Cashed In the card for guaranteed Coins instead

summary.title: Lesson: Check Defenses Before Scheming
bullets: Look at target's deployed Guards and cards in hand
If target has strong defenses, Cash In for guaranteed Coins
A Foiled Scheme gives you nothing; Coins are always useful

summary.title: Open Market
bullets: Free trading between players; no turn order
John sells a Guard to Pete for 3 Coins
Conceal Coins when done to signal Open Market is over

summary.title: John's Mistake: Revealing Wealth at Auction
bullets: John spends all 6 Coins on a Gladiator at Auction
Now everyone knows he is broke; cannot bid on other cards or Hosting
Keep Coins concealed; do not reveal your wealth early

summary.title: Lesson: Budget for Hosting
bullets: Never spend all Coins at Auction; reserve 3-5 for Hosting bid
Host gains 1 Influence and controls Arena matchups
Hosting is one of the most powerful positions in the game

summary.title: Bid for Hosting
bullets: Pete bids 4, Sally bids 3, John bids 0 (broke)
Pete wins Host marker, pays Bank, will Host the Arena

summary.title: Arena Phase: Pete Hosts
bullets: Pete gains +1 Influence as Host (7 to 8)
Pete invites John and Sally to fight
Declining costs 1 Influence; Sally accepts to avoid the penalty

summary.title: Tribute and Wagers
bullets: No Favour/Champion tokens yet, so no Tribute
Players place wagers: Pete on John winning, Sally on herself, John on Injury
Max 3 Coins per wager; fighters cannot bet against themselves

summary.title: Combat Plays Out
bullets: Initiative: roll Speed dice, higher total chooses first or second
Attack vs Defence: line up Dice Strings, compare position by position
ATK > DEF = Wound; ties go to Defender
Unopposed ATK dice on 3+ = bonus Wound

summary.title: John's Mistake: Choosing Wrong Dice to Surrender
bullets: John surrendered both Wounds from DEF pool to keep ATK high
Left with only 1 DEF die, making him easy to Wound every round
Spread Wound losses across pools to stay balanced

summary.title: Victory and the Host's Power
bullets: John wins +1 Influence; Gladiator gets a Favour token
Wagers settled: Pete wins Victory bet, John loses Injury bet
Pete (Host) gives Thumbs Up, returning Sally's Gladiator safely

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: Complete turn practiced: Upkeep, Intrigue, Market, Arena
Three key lessons: check defenses before scheming, budget for Hosting, spread Wound losses
Watched John make common mistakes and learned from each one

summary.title: Before You Enter the Arena
bullets: Check out General Tips for strategy basics
Reference the Appendix for card types and special abilities
Ask your personal GM Guide via Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we get into strategy, here are the most common mistakes new players make. First, playing Schemes without checking if the target has Guards or Reactions. You lose the card and gain nothing. Second, spending all your Coins at Auction and having nothing left for the Hosting bid. Third, dumping all Wounds into one dice pool during combat, leaving your Gladiator one-dimensional and easy to finish off. Fourth, ignoring your Hand Size and being forced to discard valuable cards at the end of your Intrigue turn. Fifth, neglecting your Slave economy and running out of Coins to pay for Gladiators during Upkeep. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the biggest beginner traps.

walkthrough.title: The Coin Economy
walkthrough.text: Coins are the lifeblood of Spartacus. You need them for everything: paying Gladiator upkeep, bidding at Auction, bidding for Hosting, placing wagers, and bribing other players. Slaves generate Coins during Upkeep. Having two or three Slaves means a steady income of 2-3 Coins per turn on top of whatever you earn from Cashing In cards or winning wagers. Never underestimate the value of a good Slave portfolio. That steady income is what funds your long-term plans.

walkthrough.title: Guard Management
walkthrough.text: Guards are your defense against Schemes. You can hold them in your hand for surprise defense or deploy them face up on the table as Assets. Keeping Guards in hand hides your defenses but takes up Hand Size space. Deploying them on the table is visible but frees up hand space and makes opponents think twice about targeting you. The ideal is a mix: one or two visible Guards to deter casual Schemes, plus one in hand for emergencies. Even visible Guards are only a 50/50 chance to stop a Scheme, so sometimes bluffing is just as effective.

walkthrough.title: When to Play Schemes vs. Cash In
walkthrough.text: Every Intrigue Card has a Coin Value at the bottom. Sometimes Cashing In a Scheme for Coins is smarter than trying to play it. Consider Cashing In when: your target has strong defenses, you need Coins for the upcoming Auction or Hosting bid, or the Scheme's effect is marginal. Consider playing Schemes when: your target has no Guards showing, the Scheme's effect is game-changing like gaining Influence or forcing a Gladiator discard, or you are close to 12 Influence and need every edge. A guaranteed 2 Coins from Cashing In beats a 50/50 shot at an effect that might not even matter.

walkthrough.title: The Hosting Advantage
walkthrough.text: Being the Host is one of the most powerful positions in the game. You gain 1 Influence automatically. You choose who fights in the Arena, which means you can pit your rivals against each other or force a weak opponent to risk their best Gladiator. You can solicit bribes for Invitations. And after the fight, you decide Thumbs Up or Thumbs Down, giving you life-or-death power. Always budget Coins for the Hosting bid. Winning the Host marker two or three turns in a row can snowball your Influence significantly.

walkthrough.title: Arena Strategy: When to Accept Invitations
walkthrough.text: Declining an Arena Invitation costs 1 Influence, which hurts. But sometimes losing 1 Influence is better than losing a Gladiator. Before accepting, consider: is your Gladiator likely to win? If not, can you equip them well enough to survive? Is there a wager you can win even if you lose the fight? Sometimes accepting a fight you might lose is worth it if you can bet on Injury or Decapitation and hedge your losses. Also, having Favour tokens on your Gladiator means you earn Tribute Coins just for fighting, win or lose.

walkthrough.title: Combat Tip: Dice Surrender Strategy
walkthrough.text: When your Combatant takes Wounds, choosing which dice to surrender is critical. Here is a simple framework. In the early rounds of a fight, keep Defence high to survive longer. In later rounds when you need to finish the fight, keep Attack high. Always keep at least 2 Speed dice so you can maneuver. Remember the Rule of One: no pool goes below 1 until all pools are at 1. A balanced Combatant with 2 of each die is often more dangerous than one with 4 Attack but 1 of everything else.

walkthrough.title: Negotiation and Deal-Making
walkthrough.text: Spartacus is as much a negotiation game as it is a strategy game. Coins can change hands at any time with no restrictions. Use this to your advantage. Pay players to support your Schemes. Bribe the Host for favorable Invitations. Sell information about your hand. Make deals for future turns. But remember the spirit of the game: promises are not binding. You can agree to support someone's Scheme and then play a Reaction to Foil it. You can promise Thumbs Up and then give Thumbs Down. Your reputation matters though, because players with a track record of betrayal will find fewer allies.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't play Schemes blindly; check for Guards and Reactions first
Don't spend all Coins at Auction; budget for Hosting bid
Don't dump all Wounds into one dice pool; stay balanced
Don't ignore Hand Size; plan which cards to keep or Cash In
Don't neglect Slaves; they fund everything through Coin income

summary.title: The Coin Economy
bullets: Slaves = steady income (1 Coin each per Upkeep)
Gladiators = ongoing expense (1 Coin each per Upkeep)
2-3 Slaves provides a reliable income base each turn
Coins fund Auctions, Hosting bids, wagers, and bribes

summary.title: Guard Management
bullets: Guards in hand: hidden defense but use Hand Size space
Guards on table: visible deterrent, frees hand space
Ideal mix: 1-2 deployed Guards + 1 in hand for emergencies
Guard success is 50/50 (4-6 on die); bluffing can be equally effective

summary.title: When to Play Schemes vs. Cash In
bullets: Cash In when: target has Guards, you need Coins, effect is marginal
Play Schemes when: target is unprotected, effect is game-changing
Guaranteed 2 Coins often beats a risky Scheme attempt
Near 12 Influence: every Scheme matters more

summary.title: The Hosting Advantage
bullets: +1 Influence, Arena control, bribe collection, life-or-death power
Always budget 3-5 Coins for the Hosting bid
Consecutive Hosting can snowball Influence quickly
Host chooses matchups: pit rivals against each other

summary.title: Arena Strategy: When to Accept Invitations
bullets: Declining costs 1 Influence; sometimes worth paying
Accept if your Gladiator can win or survive to Yield
Favour tokens earn Tribute Coins just for fighting
Hedge losses by betting on Injury or Decapitation if likely to lose

summary.title: Combat Tip: Dice Surrender Strategy
bullets: Early rounds: prioritize keeping Defence to survive
Late rounds: prioritize keeping Attack to finish the fight
Always keep at least 2 Speed dice for maneuverability
Rule of One: no pool below 1 until all are at 1; stay balanced

summary.title: Negotiation and Deal-Making
bullets: Coins can change hands at any time; use bribes and deals freely
Promises are not binding; betrayal is legal but has social cost
Reputation matters: chronic betrayers find fewer allies
Information is currency: sell knowledge about your hand or plans

walkthrough.title: The Slave Economy Engine
walkthrough.text: One of the strongest long-term strategies in Spartacus is building a Slave economy. Each Ready Slave earns 1 Coin per Upkeep, and the income is cumulative. Three or four Slaves means 3-4 Coins every single turn with no effort. This steady income funds Auction bids, Hosting bids, and wager placement without ever needing to Cash In valuable Intrigue Cards. In the early game, prioritize acquiring Slaves at Auction, even over mediocre Gladiators. A Slave paying for itself in two turns is a better investment than a weak Gladiator costing you 1 Coin per turn.

walkthrough.title: Gladiator Stable Management
walkthrough.text: You need Gladiators to fight in the Arena, but maintaining them is expensive. The key is quality over quantity. One elite Gladiator with strong stats and a good special ability is worth more than three mediocre ones. Keep your Gladiator count to two or three at most, sell or trade the rest, and invest the saved upkeep Coins into other resources. When evaluating a Gladiator, look at the total dice count across all three pools and their special ability. A Gladiator with doubles-cause-auto-wound is far more dangerous than one with raw stats.

walkthrough.title: Equipment Multiplication
walkthrough.text: Equipment Cards are force multipliers in combat. A Weapon that grants attack re-rolls, Armour that grants defence re-rolls, and Special equipment like a Net for auto-winning Initiative can turn a mediocre Gladiator into a champion. Only one of each type can be used per match, but having options lets you tailor your Combatant's loadout to the opponent. Equipment stays with you even if your Gladiator dies, so investing in Equipment has lasting value. In tournaments at 12 Influence, Equipment Coin Values count toward tiebreakers.

walkthrough.title: The Hosting Cycle Strategy
walkthrough.text: Hosting is the most reliable way to gain Influence in Spartacus. The Host gains 1 Influence guaranteed, plus they can engineer favorable Arena matchups and collect bribes. A strong strategy is to win the Hosting bid every other turn by saving Coins specifically for it. At 7-8 Influence, you might outbid everyone with 5-6 Coins. The other players split their budgets between Assets and Hosting, while you focus purely on winning the Host marker. Two consecutive Hosting turns gives you 2 Influence plus any Arena victories you engineer.

walkthrough.title: Scheme Timing and Support Manipulation
walkthrough.text: Advanced Intrigue play is about timing. High-Influence Schemes like gaining Influence or forcing opponents to discard Gladiators are most powerful when played at the right moment. If you are at 10 Influence, playing a +1 Influence Scheme puts you at 11, one step from victory. But your opponents will panic and target you hard. Consider instead playing the Scheme on the same turn you expect to Host and win an Arena match, stacking multiple Influence gains in one phase. Also, you can manipulate Support by offering to support a rival's Scheme against another rival, then Foiling it with a Reaction. You keep your Support promise but still stop the Scheme.

walkthrough.title: Champion Path to Victory
walkthrough.text: Building a Champion is a powerful but high-risk strategy. A Gladiator needs three Arena victories to earn their third Favour token and become a Champion. Owning a Champion gives you +1 Influence and earns 6 Coins in Tribute each Arena Phase. The risk is that losing a Champion costs you 1 Influence, and opponents will target your Champion with Schemes and unfavorable matchups. Protect your Champion-candidate Gladiator with Equipment, and try to get them matched against weaker opponents. If you can safely earn Champion status, the ongoing Influence and Coin benefits are enormous.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Timing
walkthrough.text: The critical strategic question in Spartacus is when to push for 12 Influence. Push too early and everyone gangs up on you with Schemes, bad matchups, and bidding wars. Push too late and someone else beats you to it. The ideal approach is to sit at 10-11 Influence and have multiple paths to gain the final 1-2 Influence in a single phase. For example, have a strong Scheme in hand, a good Gladiator ready for Arena, and enough Coins for the Hosting bid. If one path is blocked, you have backups. The player who reaches 12 with the least warning usually wins.

walkthrough.title: Arena Betting as Income
walkthrough.text: Wagers are often overlooked as a Coin source, but savvy betting can generate significant income. Victory wagers pay 1-to-1, but Injury and Decapitation wagers pay 2-to-1. If you can read a matchup well, betting 3 Coins on the likely outcome nets you 3-6 Coins back plus your original stake. Pay attention to Equipment loadouts and Gladiator stats before betting. A fight between two high-Attack, low-Defence Gladiators is more likely to end in Injury or Decapitation. Two tanky Gladiators will probably end in a clean Victory. Consistent smart betting can fund your entire strategy.

walkthrough.title: Defensive Play: Guard Stacking
walkthrough.text: When you are near 12 Influence, every other player will target you with Schemes. The counter is to stack Guards both on the table and in your hand. Each Guard gives you a 50% chance to Foil a Scheme, and you can attempt multiple Guards against the same Scheme. Two Guards mean roughly a 75% chance of stopping any Scheme. Three Guards give you about 87.5%. Buying Guards at Open Market and deploying them visibly sends a strong message: Scheme me at your own risk. Combine Guard stacking with a low profile until you are ready to sprint for 12.

walkthrough.title: Kingmaker Awareness
walkthrough.text: In a multiplayer game, the player who controls the balance of power is often not the leader but the kingmaker. If two players are at 10-11 Influence, the player at 7 with a key Reaction card or Support capability determines who wins. Be aware of your own kingmaker position and leverage it for maximum benefit. Charge heavily for Support. Threaten to Foil Schemes unless paid off. Use your Hosting power to engineer matchups that benefit you. And if you are the one near 12, recognize who the kingmaker is and buy their cooperation before they sell it to someone else.

summary.title: The Slave Economy Engine
bullets: Each Ready Slave = 1 Coin per Upkeep, cumulative
3-4 Slaves = reliable income without Cashing In cards
Prioritize Slave acquisition early over mediocre Gladiators
A Slave pays for itself in 2 turns

summary.title: Gladiator Stable Management
bullets: Quality over quantity: 2-3 elite Gladiators max
Each extra Gladiator costs 1 Coin/turn in upkeep
Evaluate total dice count + special ability, not just ATK
Sell or trade weak Gladiators to fund better investments

summary.title: Equipment Multiplication
bullets: 1 Weapon + 1 Armour + 1 Special per match
Re-roll abilities and auto-wound powers are top-tier
Equipment persists even when Gladiators die
Equipment Coin Values count in 12-Influence tournament tiebreakers

summary.title: The Hosting Cycle Strategy
bullets: Host = +1 Influence guaranteed + Arena control + bribe income
Save Coins specifically for Hosting bids; aim to Host every other turn
5-6 Coins typically wins the bid in mid-game
Consecutive Hosting snowballs Influence rapidly

summary.title: Scheme Timing and Support Manipulation
bullets: Stack multiple Influence gains in one phase (Scheme + Host + Arena)
Time +1 Influence Schemes for maximum impact near endgame
Offer Support then Foil with a Reaction for advanced disruption
The element of surprise is critical near 12 Influence

summary.title: Champion Path to Victory
bullets: 3 Favour tokens = Champion (+1 Influence, 6 Coins Tribute)
Losing a Champion = -1 Influence; high-risk, high-reward
Protect Champion candidates with Equipment and favorable matchups
Ongoing Tribute income from Champion is enormous

summary.title: Endgame Timing
bullets: Sit at 10-11 Influence with multiple paths to 12
Push too early = gang-up; push too late = someone else wins
Have Scheme, Gladiator, and Hosting bid all ready at once
The player who reaches 12 with least warning usually wins

summary.title: Arena Betting as Income
bullets: Victory: 1-to-1; Injury/Decapitation: 2-to-1 payout
Read matchups: high-ATK/low-DEF fights lean toward Injury
3 Coins on correct Injury bet = 6 Coins + 3 returned
Consistent smart betting funds your entire strategy

summary.title: Defensive Play: Guard Stacking
bullets: Each Guard = 50% Foil chance; multiple attempts per Scheme
2 Guards = ~75% stop chance; 3 Guards = ~87.5%
Deploy Guards visibly to deter Schemes; keep 1 hidden
Essential when sitting near 12 Influence

summary.title: Kingmaker Awareness
bullets: The player at 7 Influence with key cards may decide who wins
Charge heavily for Support when multiple players are near 12
Use Hosting power and Reactions as leverage
Buy the kingmaker's cooperation before your rivals do

entries.definition: The head of a Roman House in Capua. Each player takes on the role of a Dominus, managing Gladiators, Slaves, Guards, and Coins while scheming for Influence.

The primary victory currency. Tracked on your House Card. The first player to end a phase as the sole Dominus with 12 or more Influence wins the game. Influence is gained from Hosting, winning Arena fights, playing Schemes, and owning Champions.

Your player board. Tracks your current Influence, lists your Hand Size at each Influence level, shows your starting assets, and details your House's special rules. There are four unique Houses, each with different strengths.

The player who presides over the Arena Games. The Host gains 1 Influence, issues Invitations, and decides Thumbs Up or Thumbs Down for defeated Combatants. The Host marker changes hands via bidding during the Market Phase.
A card that is face up and available for use. Ready cards count toward Balancing the Ledgers, can fight in the Arena, can be traded, and their special abilities can be activated.

A card that is face down and temporarily out of play. Exhausted cards cannot be used for anything: no special abilities, no combat, no trading, no counting toward Schemes. Cards are Exhausted when their special ability is used or when Injured. They Refresh during the next Upkeep.
Flipping an Exhausted card face up during Upkeep, making it Ready. Only uninjured Exhausted cards are Refreshed. Injured cards must pass a Healing Roll first.

The maximum number of Intrigue Cards you may hold at the end of your Intrigue turn. Listed on your House Card under your current Influence level. You may temporarily exceed it during your turn but must trim down before ending.
The area on your House Card where you store your Coins. Coins in your Treasury are public during Open Market but should be concealed during Auction and Hosting bids.

The shared supply of Coins. Players pay Coins to the Bank for Gladiator upkeep, Auction wins, and Hosting bids. Players receive Coins from the Bank for Slave income, successful Schemes, Cash In, and winning wagers.

A number printed on Asset and Intrigue cards. For Assets, it is the price the Bank pays when you sell. For Intrigue Cards, it is the amount you receive when Cashing In. Coin Values have no bearing on player-to-player trades.
A Gladiator or Slave selected to fight in the Arena. The same combat rules apply to both. A Combatant's card is committed to the Arena along with their Equipment.
The deck containing Gladiators, Slaves, and Equipment cards that can be acquired during the Auction. Cards are dealt face down equal to the number of players and revealed one at a time for bidding.
The deck containing Schemes, Reactions, and Guards. Players draw 3 cards at the start of each Intrigue Phase. Intrigue Cards may never be traded between players.

An Asset card representing a trained fighter. Gladiators have three attributes (ATK, DEF, SPD), may have special abilities, and cost 1 Coin per turn during Upkeep. Gladiators fight in the Arena to earn Influence and Favour.

An Asset card representing a household servant. Slaves earn 1 Coin per Ready Slave during Upkeep. Many Slaves have Intrigue-phase special abilities. Slaves can also be sent to fight in the Arena as Combatants, though they are typically weaker than Gladiators.

An Asset card that enhances a Combatant in the Arena. There are three types: Weapon, Armour, and Special. Only one of each type may be used per Arena match. Equipment stays with the Dominus even if the Combatant dies.

A type of Equipment card that enhances offensive capabilities. Examples include swords that grant ATK re-rolls, tridents with extended attack range, and weapons that cause extra wounds. Only one Weapon per Combatant per match.
A type of Equipment card that enhances defensive capabilities. Examples include helmets that grant DEF re-rolls and shields that can ignore wounds. Only one Armour piece per Combatant per match.

A type of Equipment card with unique combat effects. Examples include javelins for speed-based ranged attacks, nets for auto-winning Initiative, and items that grant extra wounds. Only one Special Equipment per Combatant per match.

An Intrigue card that lets you affect the game by gaining Influence, forcing opponents to lose Influence or assets, drawing cards, earning Coins, or other effects. Each Scheme has a required Influence level to play. Schemes can be Foiled by Reactions or Guards.

An Intrigue card played in response to events, not during your own turn. Some Reactions Foil Schemes. Others trigger during Upkeep or other specific moments. Reactions have an Influence requirement but cannot receive Support from other players.

A special type of Reaction that can only Foil Schemes targeting your own House. Discard and roll a die: 4-6 Foils the Scheme, 1-3 fails. The Guard is discarded either way. Guards can be held in hand or deployed on the table as Assets.

To cancel a Scheme completely. When a Scheme is Foiled, none of its effects take place, including any costs. The Foiled Scheme goes to the Intrigue discard pile. Foiling is done via Reaction cards or Guard attempts.

When you lack the Influence to play a Scheme, you can ask other players to lend their Influence for that one Scheme. Support does not change anyone's actual Influence. You are not required to show the Scheme or tell the truth about it. Once granted, Support cannot be revoked.
Discarding an Intrigue Card during your Intrigue turn to receive its Coin Value from the Bank. A guaranteed source of income and a way to manage Hand Size.

The first step of each turn, performed simultaneously by all players. Consists of three steps: Refresh Cards (flip uninjured Exhausted cards face up), Heal Injuries (roll for each Injured card), and Balance the Ledgers (earn Coins for Slaves, pay for Gladiators).

The second phase of each turn. All players draw 3 cards, then take Intrigue turns starting with the Host, going clockwise. On your turn you can play Schemes, Cash In cards, and use special abilities. Must end at or under Hand Size.
The third phase of each turn. Begins with Open Market (free trading), then Auction (bid on new Market Deck cards), then Bid for Hosting (bid for the Host marker).

The fourth and final phase of each turn. The Host gains 1 Influence, issues Invitations, Tribute is paid, wagers are placed, combat occurs, and the Host exercises the Power of Life and Death over the defeated Combatant.

A sub-phase of the Market Phase where players freely trade Assets and Coins with each other and the Bank. No turn order. The only time Assets can change hands between players. Ends when all players pick up and conceal their Coins.

A sub-phase of the Market Phase where new Asset cards from the Market Deck are bid on using concealed simultaneous bidding. Cards are revealed one at a time. Tied bids trigger additional rounds. All-zero bids result in a Failed Auction.

The bidding method used during Auctions and Hosting bids. Place Coins in your closed fist, extend over the table, and reveal simultaneously with all other players. Highest bid wins. Tied bidders bid again in additional rounds.

When all remaining bidders bid zero Coins in any round of bidding, the auction fails. The card is discarded and any previous bids from tied rounds are returned to players. In a Failed Hosting bid, tied bidders roll dice and highest wins.
The third step of Upkeep. Earn 1 Coin from the Bank for each Ready Slave. Pay 1 Coin to the Bank for each Ready Gladiator. If you cannot pay, discard one Gladiator per unpaid Coin.

A die roll made during Upkeep for each Injured card. 4-6: remove the Injury token and Refresh the card. 2-3: card remains Injured. 1: the Gladiator or Slave dies and is discarded to the Market discard pile.
A combat attribute represented by red dice. Rolled when making an attack. More Attack dice means more chances to wound the opponent. Some special abilities trigger on ATK doubles or triples.

A combat attribute represented by black dice. Rolled when defending against an attack. Ties go to the Defender. More Defence dice means better survival. Some abilities trigger on DEF doubles or triples.
A combat attribute represented by blue dice. Used for Initiative rolls and determines maximum hex movement per Combat Round. Some Special Equipment uses Speed dice for attacks instead of Attack dice.
The current set of dice available for a specific attribute (ATK, DEF, or SPD). As a Combatant takes Wounds, dice are surrendered from pools. When any pool reaches zero, the Combatant is defeated.

After rolling ATK or DEF dice, arrange results from highest to lowest. Compare attacker's Dice String against defender's Dice String position by position. Attack die greater than Defence die at the same position = Wound.
At the start of each Combat Round, both players roll their Speed dice and total the results. The higher total wins Initiative and chooses whether to go first or second that round. Ties are rerolled.

Damage dealt when an Attack die beats a Defence die at the same Dice String position, or when an unopposed Attack die rolls 3 or higher. The wounded Combatant must surrender one die per Wound from any pool.
When one side rolls more dice than the other, the extra dice are unopposed. Unopposed Attack dice on 3+ cause Wounds. Unopposed Defence dice are ignored (lower ones discarded first).
No Dice Pool may be reduced below 1 die until all three pools have only 1 die each. This prevents dumping all Wounds into a single attribute. Once all pools are at 1, the next Wound ends the fight.
Defeat by having one Dice Pool reduced to zero while others remain above zero. The Combatant is at the mercy of the Host. No Injury is sustained unless the Host gives Thumbs Down.
Defeat by having two Dice Pools reduced to zero. The Combatant's card is Exhausted and marked with an Injury token. The card remains Exhausted until the Injury is Healed during a future Upkeep.
Defeat by having all three Dice Pools reduced to zero. The Combatant is killed. Their card is placed in the Market discard pile. All Equipment Cards are returned to the controlling Dominus.

A token placed on a Gladiator's card after winning an Arena match. Each Favour token earns 2 Coins in Tribute before each fight. Three Favour tokens convert into a Champion token. Executing a Combatant with Favour tokens costs the Host 1 Influence per token.

Replaces three Favour tokens when a Gladiator wins its third Arena match. A Champion earns 6 Coins Tribute before each fight. Owning a Champion grants +1 Influence; losing one costs -1 Influence. Champions may not be executed (Thumbs Down).

The Host issues Invitations one at a time to fill two spots in the Arena. An invited player must accept or decline. Declining costs 1 Influence. The Host may invite themselves and may solicit bribes for favorable or avoided Invitations.
Payment made by the Bank to owners of Combatants before wagers are placed. 2 Coins per Favour token, 6 Coins per Champion token on the committed Combatant.
The Host's merciful gesture after a fight. The defeated Combatant and their Equipment are returned to their owner. No further penalty beyond the combat result.

The Host's ruthless gesture after a fight. The defeated Combatant is executed and placed in the Market discard pile. The Host loses 1 Influence per Favour token on the executed Combatant. Champions may never be executed.
The Host's authority to decide the fate of a defeated Combatant with Thumbs Up or Thumbs Down. The Host may solicit bribes from players who want to influence the decision.

A bet that a specific Combatant will win the Arena match. Pays 1-to-1. Place 1-3 Coins on the Victory section of the Betting Board capped with your House token. Players in the fight cannot bet against their own Combatant.
A bet that the Arena match will end with one Combatant being Injured (2 Dice Pools at zero). Pays 2-to-1. Place 1-3 Coins on the Injury section of the Betting Board.
A bet that the Arena match will end with one Combatant being Decapitated (all 3 Dice Pools at zero). Pays 2-to-1. Place 1-3 Coins on the Decapitation section of the Betting Board.

When two or three dice in an ATK or DEF roll show the same number. Many Gladiator special abilities trigger on doubles or triples, causing auto-wounds, auto-blocks, or other effects. Each die can only count toward one set.
A Wound dealt automatically, bypassing the normal Dice String comparison. Triggered by certain Gladiator special abilities when rolling doubles or triples on ATK dice.
An automatic block that prevents a Wound, bypassing the normal Dice String comparison. Triggered by certain Gladiator special abilities when rolling doubles or triples on DEF dice.
Most attacks require adjacent hex positioning. Some Equipment grants extended Attack Range (e.g., Attack Range 2 for a Trident), allowing attacks from farther away on the hex grid.
An attack that uses Speed (blue) dice instead of Attack (red) dice. Granted by certain Equipment like the Javelin. Replaces the normal attack for that round, not in addition to it.

The ability to re-roll one die during combat, granted by certain Equipment or Gladiator special abilities. You choose which die to re-roll, and only the final result counts. Re-rolls apply to your own dice only, unless a card states otherwise.
Some Equipment allows you to negate one Wound after Dice Strings are compared but before surrendering dice. Effectively reduces the Wound count by one.
A Special Equipment card that allows your Combatant to automatically win the Initiative roll for one Combat Round by Exhausting the Net. No Initiative dice are rolled that round.
A Slave special ability usable during the Market Phase. After Auction cards are dealt face down, Exhaust the Informant to look at one of the face-down cards before bidding begins.
A Slave special ability usable during the Intrigue Phase. Exhaust the Spy to look at one random card in a chosen opponent's hand.
A Slave special ability usable during the Intrigue Phase. Exhaust the Skilled Slave to gain 1 Coin from the Bank.
A card ability that allows you to re-roll a failed Healing Roll during Upkeep, giving Injured Gladiators and Slaves a second chance at recovery.
A Gladiator special ability where ATK doubles cause one automatic wound to the opponent, in addition to any normal Wounds from the Dice String comparison.
A Gladiator special ability where DEF doubles cause one automatic block, negating one Wound from the opponent's attack.
A Gladiator special ability that allows rolling SPD (blue) dice instead of DEF (black) dice when defending. Useful when the Gladiator has more Speed dice than Defence dice.

Guard cards (and some other cards) have a Deployment ability allowing them to be placed on the table as an Asset at any time. Once deployed, Guards cannot be returned to your hand. Deployed Guards can be sold or traded during Open Market.

When two or more players end a phase at 12 Influence simultaneously, they enter a Tournament of Arena fights to determine the winner. In a two-player tie, one match decides it. In a three-player tie, the two lowest Gladiator+Equipment Coin Values fight first, then the winner fights the remaining player.
A game type where all players start at 7 Influence. Recommended for first-time players. Usually takes under two hours.
A game type where all players start at 4 Influence. The default game length. Usually takes two to three hours.
A game type where all players start at 1 Influence. The longest format, often running over three hours as each House rises from obscurity.
